message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 7
Recent changes: Switched Math.round to Math.ceil with toFixed(10) guard for spec-faithful "round in MHPCO favor"

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the architecture notes and the current state of the implementation and test files.

content.content:
architecture-notes.md
eslint.config.mjs
example-mapping
metrics.json
node_modules
package.json
pnpm-lock.yaml
prompt.md
run.log
src
tdd-journal.md
tsconfig.json
vitest.config.ts

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- No refactoring (justified): hardcoded `{ results: [{ premium: 115 }] }` is canonical "fake it" baby step; mass=2 is minimal; no duplication yet to extract; `runScenario` name matches contract.
5	
6	## After cycle 2
7	- Change: extracted module constants `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER = 1.1`, `ADMIN_FEE = 5`, and `BASE_PRICE: Record<string, number> = { sword: 100, amulet: 60 }`; replaced ternary `itemType === "amulet" ? 71 : 115` with formula `Math.round(BASE_PRICE[itemType] * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE`; destructured `steps` from cast scenario.
8	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent) > APP. Opaque computed literals 71/115 hid the spec's pricing model (base × first-insurance loading + admin fee); now the domain is visible. `Math.round` neutralises IEEE-754 drift (100*1.1 = 110.000…01) for the integer-premium contract; spec uses explicit `ceil(...)` only on discounted prices.
9	- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE` lookup table (extend with new item types: staff, potion, rune, moonstone). `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER` (the 1.10 loading for customers with no prior contract). `ADMIN_FEE` (the flat +5 added after all multipliers).
10	
11	## After cycle 3
12	- Change: added `staff: 80` to `BASE_PRICE`; renamed `itemType` binding to `item` (now `const item = steps[0].items[0]`, then `BASE_PRICE[item.type]`).
13	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). Upcoming tests attach modifiers (`cursed`, `enchantment`) to the same item object; naming the whole item rather than just its `type` field prepares the binding to carry those properties without rework. Mass effectively unchanged (~20). Resisted extracting `priceForItem`/`quotePremium` helpers — speculative without a duplication driver (Rule 4).
14	- Naming established: `item` as the unit modifiers attach to (will gain `.cursed`, `.enchantment` shortly).
15	
16	## After cycle 4
17	- No refactoring (justified): `potion: 40` test absorbed entirely by the cycle-2 `BASE_PRICE` lookup table — zero edits to `runScenario` body. No new conditional, no duplication, no naming drift; mass unchanged (~37). Resisted extracting `priceForItem(item)` / `quotePremium(...)` again — still speculative; the cursed/enchantment/loyal-customer/multi-item tests next in line will provide the real driver for where pricing logic should live, and premature extraction would lock the shape before those forces appear.
18	
19	## After cycle 5
20	- Change: renamed local binding `cursedMult` → `cursedMultiplier`; structure of `runScenario` body unchanged (pipeline: base lookup → item-modifier multiplier → first-insurance loading + admin fee).
21	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). Module constants are spelled out in full (`FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER`, `CURSED_MULTIPLIER`, `ADMIN_FEE`); the local mirrored their role so it should mirror their spelling. Next cycle adds `enchantmentMultiplier` — having two siblings with consistent naming will make the "apply item modifiers" stage read uniformly and signal the extraction point cleanly when a third modifier arrives. Resisted extracting `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` now: only one modifier exists, so there is no duplication yet (Rule 3 needs a driver; Rule 4 says don't add an abstraction until forced). Mass change negligible (~34, binding name length is free).
22	- Naming established: locals that mirror module-level `*_MULTIPLIER` constants use the full word `Multiplier`, not `Mult`.
23	
24	## After cycle 6
25	- Change: added `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER = 1.3`; extracted helper `itemModifierMultiplier(item: Item): number` that combines `cursed` and high-enchantment factors (each `<cond> ? MULTIPLIER : 1`, multiplied together); renamed `itemBase` → `itemPrice`; `runScenario` body collapses to base lookup → item-modifier multiplier → first-insurance loading + admin fee, with the modifier stage now a single named call.
26	- Rationale: Rule 3 (No Duplication) finally has a driver — the second sibling modifier landed in cycle 6, matching the cycle-5 prediction, so the speculative extraction resisted then is now justified. Rule 2 (Reveal Intent): naming the "apply all item-attribute multipliers" stage makes the pricing pipeline scannable; `itemPrice` is more accurate than `itemBase` once modifiers have been applied. APP rose slightly (~36 → ~38) from the function-call frame, but Rule 2 > APP per project conventions. Cycle 7 (cursed AND enchantment 5) will exercise both branches together with zero edits to `runScenario` body — both factors will simply multiply inside the helper. Cycle-7's `ceil(...)` in the spec formula (220 vs would-be-rounded 220) will need attention: current `Math.round` may give same answer for that specific test by coincidence (100×1.5×1.3×1.1 = 214.5 → round→215, ceil→215; +5 = 220 either way... actually round(214.5)=214 banker's rounding in some langs, but JS Math.round = 215; ceil = 215; both yield 220), so cycle 7 may pass with no change; cycle 9 (`ceil(60 × 0.80 × 1.10) + 5 = 58`; product = 52.8, both round and ceil → 53, +5 = 58 — also coincidence-passes). The real ceil-vs-round driver only appears when loyalty discount lands on a non-`.5` fractional that diverges; defer the round→ceil switch until a test actually fails.
27	- Naming established: `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` is THE place to add new item-attribute multipliers (dragon-material reimbursement in claims will live elsewhere — claim-side, not premium-side; this helper is premium-only). `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER` (the >=5 threshold version; if a "low-enchantment" tier ever appears, rename accordingly).
28

content.text:
All 7 tests passing. Now let me proceed with the Refactor phase.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (First Priority)

Cycle 7 introduced the cursed AND enchantment case and required switching `Math.round` to `Math.ceil` with a `.toFixed(10)` IEEE-754 guard.

**Current names and their fit:**

- `runScenario` — still the contract name, fits.
- `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` — accurate: returns combined modifier factor. Fits.
- `itemPrice` — accurate: base × item modifiers. Fits.
- `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER`, `CURSED_MULTIPLIER`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER`, `ADMIN_FEE`, `BASE_PRICE` — all reveal intent.
- `cursed` and `highEnchantment` (locals in helper) — describe the modifier each branch produces. Fits.

**Smell on the premium line:** `Math.ceil(Number((itemPrice * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER).toFixed(10))) + ADMIN_FEE` is dense, mixes three concerns (apply first-insurance loading, round in MHPCO's favour, add admin fee), and the `.toFixed(10)` / `Number(...)` hack is a domain-significant invariant ("MHPCO rounds up, but only when the cent value is genuinely above an integer — IEEE-754 drift shouldn't bump you up a euro") that is currently unnamed.

**Decision:** Extract a named helper `ceilInMhpcoFavor(value)` that encapsulates the round-up-with-drift-guard rule. This is Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — the toFixed(10) guard is currently a magic incantation; naming it as a domain rule makes the spec phrase "round in MHPCO favor" appear in the code. Will also be reused by every test that has a non-integer cent value (loyalty discounts in cycles 8–9, rune `ceil(25 × 1.10) = 28` in cycle 10, etc.) — the duplication driver is imminent and a centralised round point now avoids inlining the same toFixed dance.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

Current `runScenario` (lines 24-32):
```
const { steps } = scenario as Scenario;           // bindings:2 (steps, scenario-cast — count cast binding 1) = 2
const item = steps[0].items[0];                   // bindings:1, constants:2 (0,0), invocations:2 (index) = 1+2+2 = treat indices as invocations
const itemPrice = BASE_PRICE[item.type] * itemModifierMultiplier(item);  // bindings:2, invocations:3 (index, call, *) = 5
const premium = Math.ceil(Number((itemPrice * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER).toFixed(10))) + ADMIN_FEE;
  // bindings:3 (itemPrice, FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER, ADMIN_FEE), constants:1 (10), invocations:5 (ceil, Number, toFixed, *, +) = 3+1+10
return { results: [{ premium }] };                // bindings:1 = 1
```

Rough total for `runScenario` body: ~37 (matches cycle 6 estimate of ~38 plus ~3 added by toFixed dance in cycle 7).

## Step 3: Apply Refactoring

Extract `ceilInMhpcoFavor`:

content.text:
Task list is healthy — task #6 (cycle 7) is already marked completed and #7 (next cycle) is properly pending. No update needed from the Refactor agent; the next Red call will pick up task #7.

All 7 tests still pass. Tests stay green.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

New `runScenario` (lines 33-39):
- `const { steps } = scenario as Scenario;` — unchanged
- `const item = steps[0].items[0];` — unchanged
- `const itemPrice = BASE_PRICE[item.type] * itemModifierMultiplier(item);` — unchanged
- `const premium = ceilInMhpcoFavor(itemPrice * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE;`
  - bindings:3, invocations:3 (call, *, +) = 6
  - vs. before: bindings:3, constants:1 (10), invocations:5 (ceil, Number, toFixed, *, +) = 9

`runScenario` body mass reduced by ~3 (one constant + two invocations moved out).

New `ceilInMhpcoFavor` helper:
- bindings:1 (value), constants:1 (10), invocations:3 (ceil, Number, toFixed) = 5

Net for the module: roughly unchanged in total (the dance is moved, not eliminated), but `runScenario` reads cleaner and the round-up rule is named.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied:**
- Extracted `ceilInMhpcoFavor(value)` to name the "round up in MHPCO's favour with IEEE-754 drift guard" rule (Rule 2: Reveals Intent).
- `runScenario` premium line is now a single, scannable formula matching the spec wording: `ceilInMhpcoFavor(itemPrice × first-insurance loading) + admin fee`.
- Total module mass roughly unchanged; the toFixed(10) dance is centralised so every future ceil site (loyalty discount cycles 8–9, rune `ceil(25 × 1.10)` cycle 10, four-runes cycle 12) calls one named function instead of inlining the incantation.

**Rule priority check:** Rule 1 (tests pass) — verified, all 7 green. Rule 2 (intent) — improved. Rule 3 (DRY) — pre-emptively prepared; current code has only one ceil site, but the cycle-6 architecture note explicitly forecast cycles 8–9 needing a real ceil-vs-round driver, so a named helper anchors that growth. Rule 4 (fewest elements) — added one function (4 LOC), worth the clarity.

## Step 8: Append to Architecture Notes

input.old_string: - Naming established: `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` is THE place to add new item-attribute multipliers (dragon-material reimbursement in claims will live elsewhere — claim-side, not premium-side; this helper is premium-only). `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER` (the >=5 threshold version; if a "low-enchantment" tier ever appears, rename accordingly).
input.new_string:
- Naming established: `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` is THE place to add new item-attribute multipliers (dragon-material reimbursement in claims will live elsewhere — claim-side, not premium-side; this helper is premium-only). `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER` (the >=5 threshold version; if a "low-enchantment" tier ever appears, rename accordingly).

## After cycle 7
- Change: extracted `ceilInMhpcoFavor(value: number): number` that wraps `Math.ceil(Number(value.toFixed(10)))`; `runScenario` premium line collapses to `ceilInMhpcoFavor(itemPrice * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE`.
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). The spec phrase "round in MHPCO's favour" now appears in the code; the `.toFixed(10)` drift guard (which prevents IEEE-754 noise like `100 * 1.10 = 110.00000000000001` from bumping mathematically-integer prices up a euro) is no longer a magic incantation inlined mid-formula. Module mass roughly unchanged: the `runScenario` body lost ~3 (one constant + two invocations) which moved into the helper. Pre-positions for cycles 8–9 (loyalty discount: `100 × 0.80 × 1.10 = 88`, integer; `60 × 0.80 × 1.10 = 52.8`, non-integer needing real ceil) and cycle 10 (rune: `25 × 1.10 = 27.5`, ceil → 28) — every one of those is a `ceilInMhpcoFavor(...)` call now, no further inlining.
- Naming established: `ceilInMhpcoFavor(value)` is THE round-up gate for any euro-denominated price after multiplicative steps; use it for the final premium computation and any nested ceiling steps the spec calls out. ADMIN_FEE is added AFTER the ceiling, not inside it (spec: ceil applies to the priced value, then flat +5).

content.text:
## Step 7: Refactor Phase Complete

**Refactoring Applied**: Extracted `ceilInMhpcoFavor(value)` helper to name the "round up with IEEE-754 drift guard" domain rule.

**Mass Change**: `runScenario` body reduced by ~3 (one constant + two invocations relocated to named helper); total module mass roughly unchanged but with intent now visible.

**Tests**: All 7 passing (17 todos remain). Tests stay green.

**Rule Applied**: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — the spec phrase "round in MHPCO's favour" now appears literally in the code. Pre-positions for upcoming loyalty-discount and rune cycles where the ceil branch genuinely diverges from round.

**Naming Decision**: Kept `runScenario`, `itemModifierMultiplier`, `itemPrice` — all still fit. Added one new name: `ceilInMhpcoFavor`.
